title: practical english usage - Part 302
content:
We   say that something is between two people, things, or groups of things.

1  between          two 403 between         and   among               ______ For (he structure had better, ►    77.

She's gone to Hungary - or rather, Poland. (     ... or better, Poland?) We   do not normally use better to correct mistakes.

2 correcting        mistakes Don't start work again until you're completely better.

or quite (unlike other comparative adjectives).

When  better means ‘ recovered from an illness’ , it can be used with completely 1  'recovered' 402 better Bet is irregular (bet - bet - bet).

He won, but she never bought him  the dinner.

My father bet my mother dinner at the Ritz that she would marry him.

I bet you £5  it doesn't rain this week.

the person with whom  the bet is made, and the money or thing that is bet.

When  bet is used to talk about real bets, it can be followed by two objects: 3 two    objects I bet (you) the Conservatives (will) lose.

I bet (you) they don't come this evening (   I bet (you) they won't come.. .) After I bet (you), we  often use a present tense to refer to the future.

Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  404  big, large and great There was a large error in the accounts.

We're thinking of giving your company a very large order.

and proportions.

Large is used with countable abstract nouns referring to quantities, amounts You're making a big mistake.  Big bargains for weekend shoppers!

Big can be used with countable abstract nouns in an informal style.

H er work showed a great improvement last year.

You are making a great mistake.

His behaviour caused great annoyance. (     big/large annoyance) not I have great respect for her ideas. (     big/large respect) touch, etc.

Great is common with abstract nouns - the names of things you cannot see, 3  abstract      nouns:    usually     great Do you think Napoleon was really a great man?

Another meaning is 'famous' or 'important'.

Great clouds of smoke rose above the burning cathedral.

And it can also be used with meanings like 'large and impressive'.

I've just got a great new bike.

style, it is often used with concrete nouns to mean 'wonderful'.

Great is not normally used simply to talk about physical size. In an informal 2 great      with    concrete       nouns It was a large house, situated near the river.

She is a small woman, but she has very large hands.

Get your big feet off my flowers.

use big and large. Big is most common in an informal style.

With concrete nouns - the names of things you can see, touch, etc - we  mostly 1  concrete       nouns:    usually     big    or  large 404 b ig,   large      and   great     _______ He has a number of criminals among his friends.

Among  the first to arrive was the ambassador.

Among  can mean 'one of, 'some of or 'included in'.

What's the difference between 'between' and 'among'?

There are enormous differences between languages.

We   normally use between after difference.

We shared the work between/among the five of us.

He divided all his money between/among his children and grandchildren.

people or groups.

We   can talk about dividing or sharing things between or among more than two 3  dividing      and   sharing;     difference They found an envelope full of money among all the rubbish.

Among  is normal before a singular (uncountable) noun.

vocabulary’  •  407  born and borne Hundreds of children are born deaf every year.               -» to be born.

To  talk about coming into the world at birth, we  use the passive expression 1 be   born 407 born      and   borne For a bit with comparative adjectives and adverbs, ►    207.

I'm not a bit tired.  ‘ Do you mind if I put some music on? ‘ Not a bit.' The  informal expression not a bit means 'not at all’ .

He's a bit of a fool, if you ask me.  I've got a bit of a problem.

similar to rather a (►  565).

A bit of ɑ  can be used before some nouns in an informal style. The  meaning is 2  a  bit    of   a (Bin  not a ltiFkindr^liUle inleresling) a bit tired a bit expensive a little (too) old the meaning is usually negative or critical.
